subject: Nephrology
title: Hyponatraemia: the algorithm in 4 steps
hook: One framework that untangles the most common electrolyte question. Swipe.

points.heading: Step 1: assess volume status
points.body: Examine the patient first. Are they hypovolaemic, euvolaemic or hypervolaemic? Every next step follows from this single decision.

points.heading: Hypovolaemic
points.body: Sodium and fluid loss: diarrhoea, vomiting, diuretics or Addison's disease. Treat the cause and replace with normal saline.

points.heading: Euvolaemic
points.body: Usually SIADH, hypothyroidism or secondary (cortisol) adrenal insufficiency. SIADH is a diagnosis of exclusion: euvolaemic, low plasma osmolality, high urine osmolality and sodium, with thyroid and adrenal causes ruled out. Treat with fluid restriction.

points.heading: Hypervolaemic
points.body: Fluid-overloaded states: heart failure, cirrhosis or renal failure. Treat with fluid and sodium restriction.

caption: Hyponatraemia is the electrolyte question examiners love, and it is simple once you start with volume status. Hypovolaemic, euvolaemic or hypervolaemic decides everything that follows. SIADH sits in the euvolaemic group, but it is a diagnosis of exclusion: low plasma osmolality with high urine osmolality and sodium, once thyroid and adrenal causes are ruled out.
